skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render React and Remotion TSX component code as MP4 videos through the inference.sh CLI and hosted render service.
skill.summary: Render Remotion videos from React component code through inference.sh.
skill.author: inference-sh-7

security_audit.summary: Static findings for network access and external commands are mostly expected for a hosted video rendering skill, but they are real risk factors. The curl-to-shell installer at SKILL.md:17 is a confirmed dangerous installation pattern, and the skill sends user-provided TSX and props to inference.sh for rendering. No evidence found of prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent, so this is not blocked, but it should not publish without review and safer install guidance.

high_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped To Shell
high_findings.description: The quick start instructs users to download a remote installer and pipe it directly into sh. This is dangerous because users execute network-delivered code before local inspection, even though the document later claims checksum verification is available.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is explicit at line 17. The install note reduces intent concerns but does not remove the execution risk.

medium_findings.title: User Code And Props Sent To External Render Service
medium_findings.description: The examples submit Remotion TSX code, configuration, and props to the inference.sh render app. This appears to be the intended function, but it can expose proprietary animation code, media references, or embedded data to a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly transmit code and render inputs to an external app. This is legitimate for the skill but creates privacy and dependency risk.

medium_findings.title: Broad External CLI Permission
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash execution for any infsh command. The scope is limited to the inference.sh CLI, but the permission can still trigger login, network calls, app runs, and other CLI behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit and broad for the infsh binary. I did not find evidence that it allows arbitrary shell commands outside that prefix.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External URLs Are Expected Dependencies
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh platform resources, an image asset, and Remotion or inference.sh documentation. They are expected for this hosted rendering workflow, but they confirm external network dependency.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and align with the stated product workflow. I did not find evidence that they are hidden exfiltration endpoints.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections do not identify use of MD5, SHA-1, or another weak algorithm. The only explicit checksum language references SHA-256, which is not a weak cryptographic hash for integrity checking.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no weak cryptographic primitive at the flagged locations. The line 29 checksum reference is specifically SHA-256.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Blocks Trigger Static Shell Findings
low_findings.description: Most shell findings are command examples in Markdown rather than code that executes automatically. They remain relevant because agents and users may follow them during setup or rendering.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are Markdown examples, not executable files. The skill can still cause those commands to be run when the user requests rendering or setup.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installer
dangerous_patterns.description: A network installer is piped directly to sh in the quick start. This pattern can execute tampered or unexpected code if the transport, domain, or install script is compromised.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is directly present in the file. It is a known supply-chain risk even when the domain appears legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Render Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented workflow invokes the infsh CLI to run a remote render app. This creates network, account, and third-party service dependencies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The CLI commands are explicit and repeated. The pattern is expected for the skill but should be disclosed before publication.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote SDK Rendering Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The Python examples submit render jobs and receive output URLs through the inference.sh SDK. This is not malicious by itself, but it expands the network execution surface.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The SDK example clearly runs the remote app and streams output. The exact SDK implementation is not present in this skill, so confidence is limited to the documented behavior.

content.user_title: Render Remotion Videos from React Code
content.value_statement: Creating videos from React animation code can require complex local setup. This skill sends Remotion TSX to inference.sh and returns rendered MP4 output.
actual_capabilities: Render React and Remotion TSX component code into MP4 video through inference.sh.
Configure output width, height, FPS, duration, and codec values.
Pass props into the rendered component for dynamic video content.
Use Remotion APIs such as useCurrentFrame, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, and Sequence.
Show CLI, Python SDK, and streaming progress workflows for remote renders.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI or SDK, network access, and a configured account.
Sends component code, props, and render settings to a hosted third-party service.
Does not include a local Remotion render implementation or hosted service source code.
Input code must be valid React and Remotion code that exports a default component.

use_cases.title: Create Motion Graphics
use_cases.description: Generate branded title cards, animated text, and short visual sequences from React component code.

use_cases.title: Automate Product Videos
use_cases.description: Render repeatable product demos or social videos with props that change titles, values, and themes.

use_cases.title: Prototype Data Videos
use_cases.description: Test animated counters, charts, and timed sequences before building a full production render pipeline.

prompt_templates.title: Render A Simple Intro
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a three-second Remotion intro with a fade-in title, then render it as a 1920 by 1080 MP4.

prompt_templates.title: Render With Custom Settings
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a square Remotion video at 1080 by 1080, 60 FPS, and five seconds long with an animated progress counter.

prompt_templates.title: Render A Prop-Driven Template
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Remotion component that accepts title, subtitle, and brand color props, then render one sample MP4.

prompt_templates.title: Render A Sequenced Animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Remotion video with three timed Sequence sections, spring movement, and interpolated opacity, then render it through inference.sh.

output_examples.input: A request for a three-second fade-in title animation.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a Remotion component, submits it to inference.sh, and returns the rendered MP4 location.

output_examples.input: A request for a square animated progress counter at 60 FPS.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates the counter animation, applies the requested render settings, and reports the video output when rendering completes.

output_examples.input: A request for a reusable branded video using props.
output_examples.output: The assistant builds a prop-driven component and renders a sample using the provided title, subtitle, and color values.

best_practices: Review generated TSX before rendering if it contains proprietary content or private media URLs.
Use manual CLI installation and checksum verification instead of piping installers directly to a shell.
Start with short durations and smaller resolutions before rendering final high-resolution videos.
anti_patterns: Do not include secrets, private tokens, or confidential customer data in component code or props.
Do not render unreviewed third-party code through a hosted service.
Do not assume the skill can render offline or without an inference.sh account.

faq.question: What does this skill render?
faq.answer: It renders React and Remotion component code as MP4 video through the inference.sh render app.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflow uses inference.sh services and returns hosted render output.

faq.question: Can I control the video format?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents width, height, FPS, duration, codec, and component props.

faq.question: Is the render performed locally?
faq.answer: No. The skill describes remote rendering through inference.sh, not local Remotion rendering.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk high?
faq.answer: The skill documents a pipe-to-shell installer and sends code to an external render service.

faq.question: Can I use this with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
